Overall, the NYSE prefers a gradual introduction of blockchain technology rather than a radical restructuring of the financial system.

▌Iran has formally responded to the "15-point plan," asserting that the US's negotiating statement is a "third deception."

March 26th news, according to Iran's Tasnim News Agency, informed sources stated that Iran formally submitted its response to the "15-point proposal" to the US last night through intermediaries and is currently awaiting a US reply. Iran's response included the following core demands: ① The enemy's terrorist aggression must cease; ② Objective conditions must be established to ensure that war will not be repeated; ③ Compensation and restitution for war losses must be clearly defined and guaranteed; ④ The ceasefire must be implemented simultaneously on all fronts and in all resistance organizations involved in the fighting throughout the region.

... He further emphasized that Iran's exercise of sovereignty over the Strait of Hormuz is its natural and legitimate right, and a guarantee to ensure the other party fulfills its commitments, which must be recognized. The source pointed out that Iran has determined that the US's negotiating statements are a "third deception" scheme, with three true objectives: ① to deceive the international community by fabricating a peaceful posture; ② to depress global oil prices; and ③ to buy time for a ground invasion from southern Iran. (Golden Ten) Market Data As of press time, according to CoinGecko data: BTC price is $68,706.90, a 24-hour change of -3.5%; ETH price is $2,057.56, a 24-hour change of -5.0%; BNB price is $628.28, a 24-hour change of -2.9%; SOL price is $86.33, a 24-hour change of... rgb(255, 0, 0);">-5.8%；

DOGE price is $0.09187, 24-hour change -4.3%；

XRP price is $1.36, 24-hour change -4.0%；

TRX price is $0.3122, 24-hour change -1.3%；

WLFI price is $0.09806, 24-hour change -5.8%；

DOGE price is $0.09187, 24-hour change ...24-hour change 24-hour change

▌Trump Reiterates: Iran is in Talks with the US

On March 26, it was learned that US President Trump stated that Iran is in talks with the US. Trump said they will wait and see if a proper agreement can be reached and the Strait of Hormuz opened. Iran must reach an agreement, otherwise it will face continued and fierce attacks from the US.

... (CCTV News) Trump Postpones Strike on Iranian Power Plants for Another 10 Days On March 27, US President Trump posted on his social media platform "Real Social," stating, "At the request of the Iranian government, I hereby declare that I will postpone the strike on [Iran's] energy facilities for another 10 days, until 8 p.m. Eastern Time on April 6, 2026." Trump also stated, "Negotiations are ongoing, and despite false and contradictory statements from fake news media and others, the negotiations are progressing very well." (CCTV International News) Blockchain Applications ... ARK announced that it will use data from the prediction market platform Kalshi to guide investment decisions and hedge risks. ARK stated that it will use Kalshi in three ways: first, to provide "continuously updated expectations" to supplement fundamental and quantitative analysis; second, to obtain real-time market expectations through indicators such as trading volume; and third, for event-driven risk management, including hedging against specific outcomes affecting portfolios as well as macroeconomic and industry risks. Cathie Wood pointed out that introducing prediction markets into institutional investment processes is a natural extension of financial research innovation, helping to quantify uncertainty and improve decision-making quality. Nick Grous, Research Director at ARK, stated that prediction markets can more purely reflect risk expectations for key economic and corporate events.

▌Google: Developing a Post-Quantum Cryptography Migration Timeline, Aiming for Completion by 2029

On March 26, Google announced its 2029 timeline, planning to complete its post-quantum cryptography (PQC) migration to ensure data security in the quantum era.

Google stated last month that it had already called for building robust security defenses for the quantum era before future quantum computers can break existing encryption algorithms. This new timeline was developed to adapt to the migration needs of the post-quantum cryptography era, taking into account factors such as the progress of quantum computing hardware development, quantum error correction technology, and quantum factorization resource calculations. Quantum computers will pose a significant threat to current cryptographic standards, especially to encryption algorithms and digital signature technologies. Encryption algorithms already face threats such as "store-first, decrypt-later" attacks; while digital signatures face future threats, requiring a transition to post-quantum cryptography before the advent of quantum computers (CRQC) with actual cryptographic threats. Therefore, we have adjusted our threat response model, prioritizing the post-quantum cryptographic migration of identity authentication services—a crucial step in cybersecurity and digital signature upgrades. We recommend that other technology teams adopt the same strategy.

Cryptocurrency

▌Coinbase and Better Launch Crypto-Backed Mortgages, Supporting BTC and USDC as Collateral

Coinbase announced a partnership with US online mortgage platform Better to launch its first compliant crypto-backed mortgage product, allowing users to use Bitcoin or USDC from their Coinbase accounts as collateral to pay for a down payment on a home. The mortgage is issued and managed by Better and has the same legal protection as traditional compliant mortgages. The product is structured as a "dual loan model": one is a standard mortgage, and the other is a down payment loan secured by crypto assets. Both have the same interest rate and term. Users only need to pay one combined monthly payment. The collateral will be held in custodian in a Coinbase Prime account and returned after the loan is repaid. The mortgage terms are unaffected by BTC price fluctuations.

▌Fannie Mae to Accept Crypto Asset-Backed Mortgages for the First Time

On March 26, according to the Wall Street Journal, Fannie Mae, a "super giant" in the U.S. mortgage market, will accept mortgages secured by crypto assets for the first time. This marks the formal inclusion of cryptocurrencies into the institution's mortgage underwriting system, allowing borrowers to use digital assets to qualify for loans or as collateral. Advantages: Expands financing channels for crypto asset holders, avoids tax liquidation, and gains recognition for digital assets in the mainstream housing finance sector. Risks: Crypto asset volatility could lead to a surge in default rates, and Fannie Mae's government backing could expose taxpayers to potential risks.

... According to Arkham data, BlackRock transferred a total of 15,405 ETH, worth approximately $32.02 million, to a Coinbase Prime address through its Ethereum exchange-traded fund ETHA; and a total of 1,133.658 BTC, worth approximately $78.83 million, to a Coinbase Prime address through its Bitcoin exchange-traded fund IBIT. On March 26, Bitcoin mining company MARA announced a $1 billion convertible senior bond repurchase program. The repurchase of the 2030 and 2031 notes is expected to be completed on March 30 and 31, 2026, respectively, subject to customary closing conditions. The repurchase transaction is expected to generate approximately $88.1 million in cash reserves for the company. The company also announced today that it sold 15,133 bitcoins between March 4 and March 25, 2026, for a total transaction value of approximately $1.1 billion. The proceeds from the bitcoin sales are expected to be used for the note repurchase transaction, with the remainder used for general corporate purposes. **Strategy CEO: Approximately 40% of MSTR Shares are Held by Retail Investors, While Retail Investors Hold Up to 80% of STRC** On March 26th, Phong Le, CEO of Bitcoin treasury company Strategy ($MSTR), stated that approximately 40% of MicroStrategy (MSTR) shares are held by retail investors, while retail investors hold up to 80% of STRC shares. **Yi Lihua: The War Is Unlikely to End in the Short Term, But There Will Definitely Be a Buying Opportunity This Year** On March 26th, Yi Lihua, founder of Liquid Capital (formerly LD Capital), published an article stating that the war is unlikely to end in the short term, and the market is waiting for further clarification of the war. However, there will definitely be a great buying opportunity this year, and it is advisable to prepare as much ammunition as possible before then. Leaving aside the crypto industry, I've recently been studying AI and have been deeply impressed, especially by the efficiency of directing AI. This isn't just a simple efficiency improvement; it's like going directly from horse-drawn carriages to the airplane era. I require all the companies I invest in to immediately learn to use and embrace AI. In the future, companies that don't use AI will be eliminated. Today, thanks to AI, all companies and individuals are basically on the same starting line. Another major wave is coming, and my goal for myself this year is to spend half my time working collaboratively with AI.

Important Economic Developments

▌European Parliament Approves EU-US Trade Agreement

On March 26, the European Parliament voted on the EU-US trade agreement, which was ultimately approved. On March 19, the European Parliament's International Trade Committee, with 29 votes in favor, resumed the legislative approval process for the EU-US trade agreement. The EU and the US are expected to reach a trade agreement in July 2025.

Golden Encyclopedia

▌Can Bitcoin Still Be Called "Digital Gold" During Geopolitical Crises?

Every geopolitical crisis causes gold prices to rise while Bitcoin prices plummet. After six tests, the claim of "digital gold" has never been supported by data. Countries hoard gold but exclude Bitcoin from their reserves. For investors, Bitcoin exhibits asymmetry: it falls with stocks but does not rise with them. Three structural asymmetries prevent Bitcoin from achieving safe-haven status: an oversupply of derivatives (market structure), the dominance of leveraged traders (participant composition), and a lack of repeatable behavioral records (behavioral accumulation).

Bitcoin is not a safe-haven asset, but it is a "crisis-useful asset," which can indeed function when borders are closed and banks fail. If these three asymmetries are reduced, Bitcoin may no longer be a replica of gold but rather a completely new "next-generation gold."

Generational shifts and the widespread application of algorithms are key factors that could accelerate this process.

data.title: Humans are not ready for AI shopping and payments.
data.description:
AI Shopping, Amazon Says No

Imagine this: You tell AI, "Find me a pair of running shoes under $120, suitable for flat feet, with the best reviews," and it automatically visits Amazon, Walmart, and Nike, compares prices, reads reviews, filters out merchant advertisements, and places the order directly. You don't need to tap the screen once during the entire process.

Isn't that wonderful? And incredibly cost-effective?

This isn't just a fantasy; it's already happening.

Perplexity launched a feature called "Comet" that works exactly like this. But Amazon quickly sued Perplexity.

On March 9, 2026, the U.S. District Court for the Western District of Washington issued a temporary injunction against Amazon, prohibiting Comet from accessing the Amazon website. A week later, the Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals suspended the injunction. Comet was reinstated, but the legal battle had only just begun. On the surface, it's a legal battle, but in reality, it's an advertising battle. This battle, ostensibly a technical dispute, is actually about a fundamental question: When a human authorizes an AI to spend money on their behalf, does the platform have the right to exclude the AI? Amazon's legal basis is the 1986 Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (CFAA). It accuses Perplexity of "intentionally bypassing" the rules by disguising Comet's identity as a legitimate Chrome browser user. This accusation has some merit. The CFAA was designed to combat hacking into computer systems, and one provision prohibits "access to a computer beyond authorized limits." Amazon's logic is that human users' authorization on Amazon is for real people; AI using a fake identity exceeds that authorization. Perplexity's rebuttal is direct: your real motive is to protect $56 billion in advertising revenue. A large portion of Amazon's profits comes from merchants purchasing "sponsored product" placements—a significant portion of the "recommendations" you see in search results are paid for by merchants. AI agents don't have eyes and aren't attracted by misleading banner ads; they only provide the truly optimal solution. For Amazon, this "eyeless" characteristic of AI directly shakes its advertising system. Interestingly, the court initially sided with Amazon. The federal district court's logic was that even if a human user authorized AI to shop on their behalf, the platform has the right to refuse service based on "non-human access." The implication is: platform authorization takes precedence over user authorization—if you want AI to enter, you must first ask the platform's permission. The Ninth Circuit Court's stay order temporarily shelved this judgment but didn't negate the logic, only stating that "the evidence is currently insufficient to uphold the injunction." The case will continue. But more noteworthy than the outcome of this lawsuit is the real problem it exposes: the entire payment system, from its inception, never considered AI as a payment provider. Current payment systems are designed for humans. Financial services, such as shopping websites, require users to undergo KYC (Know Your Customer) procedures, such as uploading passport scans, undergoing facial recognition, and sometimes even requiring printed documents for signing and a photo taken while holding the document. CAPTCHAs require you to recognize traffic lights and zebra crossings. Credit card settlements take T+1 to T+7 and incur fixed fees—the standard rate for US merchants is 2.9% plus $0.30 per transaction, with an additional 1% for international cards. This set of rules is very user-friendly for human consumers. However, for AI as the payer, all of this is completely irrelevant—its consumption logic is entirely different from that of humans. The transaction characteristics of AI agents are high frequency, extremely small amounts, and 24/7 operation. Each API call costs $0.0001, reading a single line of data costs $0.00001, while purchasing one second of GPU computing power might only cost $0.000001. With traditional credit card payments, transaction fees alone consume the entire transaction amount. This isn't a problem that can be solved by optimizing the user experience; the entire infrastructure may need to be changed. Therefore, in 2026, tech companies, financial institutions, and Web3 developers are rebuilding a payment architecture for AI. According to my understanding, this involves roughly three layers. The first layer: Identity. Previously called KYC (Know Your Customer). Now, it's called KYA (Know Your Agent). The KYA protocol needs to answer three questions: Who does this Agent represent? What are its single payment limits and validity periods? Does it have any history of defaults? Skyfire combines OAuth authorization with on-chain cryptographic credentials, issuing the AI ​​a digital passport as an "authorized agent." Merchants, seeing this passport, know that a real human user is responsible for the transaction, and can find the right person if there are any problems. F5's cloud defense system can identify legitimate AI traffic carrying KYA tokens, distinguishing it from malicious crawlers. Merchants are willing to open their checkout pages to this type of AI because they know it is "authenticated" and not there to steal data. There is also a technological foundation: TEE, Trusted Execution Environment. The role of TEE is to provide a hardware-level safe for the AI's private keys. The mainstream solution is Intel SGX or TDX, where the core logic of the AI ​​Agent runs in a physically isolated encrypted chamber. The direct purpose of this design is security—even if the cloud server is compromised, attackers cannot see the private key and cannot tamper with payment decisions. However, it solves more than just security issues. It transforms the legal concept of "authorization is not the same as transfer" into a verifiable technical fact: users grant AI the authority to spend money, but AI can only invoke signatures within preset rules; it cannot sign outside these rules. The second layer: Protocols. Machines should not simulate human clicks on web pages. The HTTP status code 402, defined in 1999, has never been used. Coinbase and Cloudflare activated it in late 2025. The workflow is simple: the AI ​​requests data from the server, the server says "This data costs 0.01 USDC", and returns a 402 status code and the receiving address; the AI ​​wallet automatically generates a cryptographic signature, attaches it to the request header and sends it out; the server verifies the signature, funds are settled in real time through the L2 network, and the data is returned directly. The whole process takes less than a second, with no redirects, no pop-ups, and no CAPTCHAs. Stripe and Visa's MPP protocol goes even further, supporting a "one-time authorization, multiple expenditures" session mode: users give the AI ​​a 24-hour valid shopping window, setting a $200 limit. All transactions within this range are automatically settled by the AI, without requiring human confirmation for each transaction. Stripe internally calls this "the money version of OAuth". The third layer: Settlement. At this level, stablecoins are the only answer. Why not bank transfers? Not PayPal? Not a points system? Because stablecoins operate on a blockchain, making them programmable. Bank transfers are batch settlements; money sent today might not arrive for two days, creating a window of opportunity, tying up funds, and posing counterparty risk. Stablecoins, on the other hand, have millisecond-level settlement times, eliminating window periods and ensuring real-time fund arrival. USDC on the Base chain has a single transaction fee of less than $0.0001, with settlement completed in 200 milliseconds. More importantly, it supports "streaming payments"—for every second of GPU computing power used by AI, the corresponding amount flows in real-time, perfectly billed based on usage. This is a fundamental change for AI commercial applications: AI service providers can charge based on actual consumption, without prepayment, locked funds, or concerns about price fluctuations during the settlement cycle. The AI's wallet isn't a human-style wallet with a mnemonic phrase; instead, it's a highly customized smart contract with three embedded security valves: Whitelist Mechanism: Funds can only flow to legitimate merchants verified by Skyfire KYA. Not a single penny can be transferred to unverified addresses. This eliminates the risk of the AI ​​being hijacked and transferring funds arbitrarily. Idempotency Protection: Prevents the agent from being charged repeatedly for the same order due to logical loops or network instability. Multiple charges for a single confirmation is a major problem in traditional payments, but it can be avoided through design in smart contracts. Circuit Breaker: This prevents the AI ​​from triggering abnormal transactions. If the Agent initiates more transactions than a preset threshold within a minute—for example, a maximum of 10 transactions per minute in a normal scenario, suddenly increasing to 1000—the contract automatically locks, all transactions are suspended, and humans are called in to investigate what happened. This is not an option, but a necessary security design.

Four Legal Issues to be Resolved

A well-designed technical solution does not automatically eliminate legal issues.

Who compensates if the AI ​​makes a mistake?

If the AI ​​misreads product information, treating the unit price as the total price and placing a $1 million order—who is liable?

Under current agency law, if the merchant has reason to believe that the AI ​​represents the user's true intentions, the contractual liability usually rests with the user. But this logic needs to be patched.

One possible solution is to force human confirmation for orders exceeding five times the user's average daily spending, even if the AI ​​has full authorization. This mechanism is called a "digital cooling-off period." Without this mechanism, such orders should be legally considered revocable. AI's Duty of Loyalty: If Perplexity's Agent deliberately avoids low-priced platforms with which it has no partnership, recommending higher-priced products for which it receives commissions—would this constitute a breach of contract? As the user's agent, AI should seek the optimal benefit under the constraints set by the user. If a service provider manipulates AI's choices through algorithms for personal gain without clearly informing the user, the user has the right to sue under the Consumer Rights Protection Law and agency law. This is not a theoretical risk. AI recommendation algorithms are inherently susceptible to contamination from "bid-for-performance" rankings. Without mandatory transparency requirements, users have no idea whether their agent is setting traps for them. How can AML penetrate AI transactions? AI's high-frequency, micro-amount payments are a natural breeding ground for money laundering—each transaction is too small and too frequent for traditional risk control models to monitor. The solution lies at the protocol layer. The x402 "Facilitator" role must fulfill KYT responsibilities, monitoring in real time whether on-chain funds are flowing to sanctioned addresses. Every stablecoin change must be traceable to the actual "carbon-based beneficiary." Once an AI system is used for illegal money laundering, not only will the company face regulatory penalties, but relevant personnel may also be held personally liable. The idea of ​​"human-machine shared responsibility" simply means that the coders cannot hide behind the company to escape responsibility. Algorithm engineers who know this code will be used for money laundering and still deliver it are no different from directly participating in a crime. This logic is not without basis in the current legal framework—the provisions on joint crimes in the Criminal Law can precisely cover this situation of "knowingly committing a crime." Who keeps your wallet safe? For AI to help humans spend money, it must hold the user's payment credentials—private keys, platform login permissions, and even biometric tokens. This scenario is progressing faster than most people imagine. Today, AI can help users buy plane tickets, book hotels, and buy running shoes; the next step is paying utilities, credit card bills, and managing daily expenses. AI holds the user's bank account password, payment password, and login permissions for various platforms. Here's a question most people haven't considered: handing over credentials to AI and handing over your rights to AI are two different things. To illustrate this with a real-life example: Giving your bank card to your children for emergency use doesn't mean you're authorizing them to freely manage every penny in your account. There are implicit boundaries, and both parties are aware of them, even if they're not explicitly written down. The same principle applies to AI holding users' payment credentials—authorizing it to spend money on your behalf doesn't mean authorizing it to spend it however it wants. However, legally, this boundary is currently ambiguous. No precedent clarifies the scope within which AI has the right to spend money on your behalf, and what limits require your confirmation. Solving this problem requires simultaneous progress on two levels. On the legal level, simply including a "limited authorization" clause in the user agreement is insufficient; ultimately, precedent and legislation are needed to provide a comprehensive framework. Currently, the legal status, liability boundaries, and scope of authorization for AI agents are not systematically defined in any country. The ongoing lawsuit between Amazon and Perplexity will likely lead to US court rulings serving as one of the earliest benchmarks. Before that, each case had to be handled individually. The same applies to regulation—the existing financial regulatory framework was designed for financial institutions, not AI service providers, and this regulatory gap won't disappear on its own. On the technical level, protocols alone aren't enough; code is also needed to enforce this restriction. TEE hardware isolation is one solution, but a larger direction deserves attention: blockchain. The core characteristic of blockchain is decentralized storage—data isn't concentrated on a single server but distributed across multiple nodes, meaning that a breach of any single point won't lead to a large-scale leak. Applying this characteristic to AI payment scenarios, user payment credentials can be stored on the blockchain, not in the AI ​​service provider's database. When AI retrieves credentials, private key fragments participate in calculations on the blockchain but are never fully extracted and exposed in memory. This means that even the AI ​​service provider itself cannot unilaterally use user funds. In this sense, both the technical and legal levels are on the same path: both are addressing the question of "who is responsible." Law relies on accountability systems, and technology relies on architectural design; both ultimately point to the same goal—ensuring that every penny spent by AI has a traceable responsible party. What lies beyond the entry point? The wall between AI and shopping, AI and payments, will inevitably be torn down, not because of court rulings, but because the trend won't be reversed by a single service agreement. The payment revolution is just the first domino. When AI can spend money on behalf of humans, it gains a passport to the real world. The following scenarios are not hard to imagine: AI negotiating salaries for you, making investment decisions for you, participating in supply chain settlements, and purchasing digital assets in the virtual world. Once these things happen, the existing civil law system, financial regulatory framework, and data ownership concepts will all face pressure for restructuring. This is not alarmist. Each generation of fundamental internet transformations gives rise to a completely new set of legal issues. The PC era had computer crime, the internet era had privacy violations and platform liability, and the mobile internet era has mobile payments and fintech regulation—in each wave, lawyers are not bystanders, but rather among the first to rush in and establish the rules. The combination of Web3 and AI is the main theme of the next generation of the internet. And Mankiw's law firm focuses precisely on the legal issues unfolding in this new world: the compliance paths for stablecoins and crypto payments, the boundaries of responsibility for AI agents, the validity of smart contracts, and the regulatory jurisdiction of Web3 projects. These issues won't become meaningful until regulatory documents are issued—they are happening now.
